Custom Shapes

Bots can take on nearly any shape imaginable.

The following steps will teach you how to upload your own shapes.

Adding Pictures

Any bot can take the form of an image. Two tags need to be set:

  • The #form tag must be set to "sprite"
  • The #formAddress tag should be the URL of an image

Usually when you see an image online, you can right-click on it and click "Copy Image Address" to get the URL for the image. You can put that URL in the #formAddress tag.

If you drag an image onto CasualOS from your computer, the image will be uploaded and a bot will be made for you.

NOTE: All images will be squished into a square in CasualOS. You can stretch them back out with #scaleX and #scaleY.

Adding Models

To make a bot look like a model, three tags need to be set:

  • #form should be set to "mesh"
  • #formSubtype should be "gltf"
  • #formAddress should be a link to the model

Note: the model you use can either be a .GLTF or .GLB file. In either case, the bot's #formSubtype tag should be set to "gltf"

You can drag a .GLTF or .GLB file from your computer onto CasualOS to upload the file to CasualOS' servers and automatically create a bot for it.

Finding Models

If you need a model to upload, check out the following resources:

  • Poly Pizza - A website for finding open source low-poly models
  • Khronos Group - A GitHub repository of sample models by the creators of the .GLTF filetype

Form Animations

Models sometimes have animations. If your model is animated, you can set its #formAnimation tag to the name of one of those animations

This model has a few animations for you to test. Try changing the model's #formAnimation to any of the following:

  • Idle
  • Walk
  • Gallop
  • Attack
  • Eating

Notice how the first letter of each is capitalized? You will need to type in into the tag that way, too.
